text:
MEDICAL ONCOLOGY FOLLOW-UP NOTE
Patient: Raymond Kowalski
Attending: Medical Oncology
Visit Type: Established Patient Follow-Up

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT
Mr. Kowalski is a 71-year-old male with metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC) presenting for follow-up after completion of 2 cycles of carboplatin + cabazitaxel. He presents today for restaging discussion and reassessment of goals of care in the setting of confirmed disease progression.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Mr. Kowalski is a 71-year-old retired steelworker with a 45 pack-year smoking history (quit at age 62) who was initially diagnosed approximately 4 years ago with de novo metastatic hormone-sensitive prostate cancer (mHSPC), Gleason 9 (5+4), Grade Group 5, with bilateral pelvic lymphadenopathy and 8 bone metastases on initial staging. He harbors a pathogenic germline BRCA2 mutation (p.E1308*), and his primary tumor demonstrated PTEN homozygous deletion, TP53 mutation, RB1 loss, and CDK12 frameshift mutation. He was initially treated with ADT plus abiraterone/prednisone with an excellent initial PSA response (187 to nadir 1.1 ng/mL). He subsequently developed castration resistance with rising PSA at approximately age 69, was transitioned to enzalutamide, which yielded a partial response for roughly 8-9 months before resistance developed. He then progressed to olaparib 300 mg BID based on his germline BRCA2 status (supported by PROfound trial data), with initial PSA response from 14.3 down to 4.8 ng/mL, but was complicated by recurrent grade 3 anemia requiring multiple transfusions, necessitating two sequential dose reductions to 200 mg BID and then 100 mg BID.

Restaging imaging at approximately 7 months on olaparib revealed new hepatic lesions (2 lesions right hepatic lobe, segments VI-VII) confirmed to be metastatic prostatic adenocarcinoma on CT-guided core biopsy, with Ki-67 of approximately 60% and focal synaptophysin positivity raising concern for early treatment-emergent neuroendocrine differentiation. Repeat NGS on the liver biopsy specimen demonstrated new AR amplification (copy number 12), AR p.L702H ligand-binding domain mutation, PIK3CA p.E545K, and AURKA amplification -- all acquired resistance alterations -- while retaining the germline BRCA2 truncating mutation without reversion. MSI remained stable and TMB was low-intermediate at 6.1 mutations/Mb.

Given the combination of visceral progression, BRCA2-positive CRPC, neuroendocrine features, and AR pathway resistance, the multidisciplinary tumor board recommended carboplatin + cabazitaxel. Olaparib was discontinued and combination chemotherapy was initiated: carboplatin AUC 4 + cabazitaxel 20 mg/m2 every 21 days with filgrastim prophylaxis and dexamethasone premedication. ADT (leuprolide acetate 22.5 mg IM q3 months) was continued.

Cycle 1 was complicated by febrile neutropenia requiring 5-day hospitalization with IV antibiotics, grade 3 anemia requiring 2 units of pRBCs, grade 2 thrombocytopenia, grade 1 peripheral sensory neuropathy, and a transient AKI (creatinine peak 2.1 mg/dL). Doses were reduced for cycle 2: cabazitaxel to 15 mg/m2, carboplatin to AUC 3.

He has now completed cycle 2. Restaging CT of the chest/abdomen/pelvis was performed and results are available for review today. The scan unfortunately demonstrates progressive disease: the two known hepatic lesions have grown slightly (from 1.2 cm and 1.0 cm to 1.4 cm and 1.3 cm) with a new 0.6 cm lesion in segment V. The right lower lobe pulmonary nodule has grown from 5 mm to 9 mm with the second nodule increasing from 4 mm to 6 mm, now raising significant concern for pulmonary metastases. The right obturator lymph node has enlarged to 2.4 cm (from 1.6 cm). PSA today is 18.6 ng/mL, up from 14.3 ng/mL at olaparib discontinuation and 11.8 ng/mL at chemotherapy initiation.

Mr. Kowalski's functional status has declined considerably over the past several months. He reports spending the majority of his waking hours in a recliner or in bed. He is no longer able to perform most activities of daily living independently and requires assistance with bathing and dressing. He has lost approximately 15 pounds over the past 5 months. He reports severe fatigue, persistent pain across multiple sites (lower back, bilateral hips, left shoulder), significantly decreased appetite, and intermittent nausea. He notes that the fatigue has been the most debilitating symptom, stating that he feels "completely wiped out" most of the time. He is currently taking oxycodone ER 20 mg every 12 hours with oxycodone IR 5 mg every 4 hours as needed for breakthrough pain; he is using 3-4 breakthrough doses per day.

His wife is present at today's visit and notes that he has also had increasing difficulty with word-finding and concentration over the past few weeks, which she attributes primarily to the opioid medications. She expresses concern about his overall trajectory and asks about further treatment options. Mr. Kowalski himself expresses that he has been thinking seriously about what he wants, stating he wants "whatever gives me more time with my family but not at the cost of going through hell."

He has not had fevers since hospitalization after cycle 1. No new neurological symptoms, no bowel or bladder dysfunction. No hemoptysis. He has had dyspnea on exertion with any activity, but denies dyspnea at rest currently.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Constitutional: Significant fatigue (grade 4 at worst, currently grade 3-4), 15 lb weight loss over 5 months, decreased appetite, occasional night sweats.
Cardiovascular: No chest pain, no palpitations, mild bilateral lower extremity edema.
Pulmonary: Exertional dyspnea, no dyspnea at rest, no hemoptysis, mild chronic cough.
Gastrointestinal: Decreased appetite, intermittent nausea (grade 1-2), constipation managed with bowel regimen, no vomiting, no hematemesis, no melena, no jaundice reported by patient (noted on exam).
Genitourinary: No hematuria, some urinary hesitancy (chronic, attributed to prostate disease/prior hormonal therapy), no obstructive uropathy symptoms acutely.
Musculoskeletal: Diffuse bone pain, worse in lumbar spine, bilateral hips, and left shoulder. Pain 5/10 overall on current opioid regimen; up to 8/10 with activity.
Neurological: No new weakness, no bowel/bladder incontinence, mild word-finding difficulty (attributed to opioids per family), no headache, no vision changes.
Dermatologic: No rash, no wound issues.
Hematologic: Ongoing fatigue and dyspnea consistent with chronic transfusion-dependent anemia.
Psychiatric: Anxious about prognosis; wife and family are present and supportive. Appropriate insight into clinical situation.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

Vital Signs: Blood pressure 108/68 mmHg (hypotension relative to prior baseline of ~130s/80s), heart rate 98 bpm, respiratory rate 18/min, temperature 37.1 degrees C, oxygen saturation 94% on room air (borderline), weight 161 lbs (down from 176 lbs at prior visit).

General: Elderly male in visible distress related to pain and fatigue. Appears cachectic and fatigued. Pallor evident. Alert and oriented x3, conversational but noticeably slowed.

HEENT: Scleral icterus mildly present. Conjunctival pallor. Dry mucous membranes. No cervical lymphadenopathy.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs. 2+ pitting edema bilateral lower extremities to mid-calf.

Pulmonary: Decreased breath sounds bilaterally at right lower lobe base. No frank consolidation or pleural friction rub. No wheezing.

Abdomen: Soft, mildly distended. Hepatomegaly appreciated - liver edge palpable approximately 3-4 cm below right costal margin, mildly tender. No splenomegaly. No ascites on clinical exam. Bowel sounds present.

Musculoskeletal: Tenderness to palpation over lumbar spine (L2, T12 levels), bilateral hip regions, and left shoulder. No obvious deformity. Reduced range of motion in all extremities limited by pain and fatigue.

Neurological: Cranial nerves intact. No focal motor deficit. Strength grossly 4/5 bilaterally in upper and lower extremities (limited by pain). Sensation to light touch intact. No signs of cord compression; no lower extremity hyperreflexia, no clonus, no Babinski.

Skin: Pale, mildly jaundiced. No rash. Prior radiation field over right proximal femur with mild hyperpigmentation.

Lymph nodes: No palpable cervical, axillary, or inguinal adenopathy.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS

Complete blood count:
- WBC: 5.1 K/uL
- ANC: 2.8 K/uL
- Hemoglobin: 7.1 g/dL (pre-transfusion; this is his third transfusion in a 2-month window)
- Hematocrit: 21.8%
- MCV: 94 fL
- Platelets: 104,000/uL

Comprehensive metabolic panel:
- Sodium: 132 mEq/L (hyponatremia, mild, likely multifactorial)
- Potassium: 4.1 mEq/L
- Bicarbonate: 22 mEq/L
- BUN: 28 mg/dL
- Creatinine: 1.9 mg/dL (eGFR approximately 36 mL/min; CKD stage 3b-4, worsening)
- Glucose: 148 mg/dL (slightly elevated, on chronic prednisone equivalent from prior)
- Total bilirubin: 1.8 mg/dL (elevated, up from 0.9 at last visit, correlating with hepatic involvement)
- Direct bilirubin: 1.1 mg/dL
- AST: 78 U/L (elevated)
- ALT: 52 U/L (elevated)
- Alkaline phosphatase: 418 U/L (markedly elevated from 287 U/L at prior visit; rising due to hepatic metastases and osseous disease)
- Total protein: 5.6 g/dL
- Albumin: 2.7 g/dL (hypoalbuminemia, significant, consistent with malnutrition/hepatic dysfunction and advanced malignancy)

Other:
- LDH: 689 U/L (markedly elevated; elevated disease burden, hepatic involvement)
- PSA: 18.6 ng/mL (rising, up from 11.8 ng/mL at initiation of current regimen)
- Testosterone: 21 ng/dL (confirmed castrate-level)
- PT/INR: 1.4 (mildly elevated, mild hepatic synthetic dysfunction)
- Uric acid: 7.1 mg/dL

---

IMAGING RESULTS

Restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis (reviewed today):

Liver: The two previously identified lesions in the right hepatic lobe (segments VI-VII) have grown -- the dominant lesion now measures 1.4 cm (previously 1.2 cm) and the second lesion measures 1.3 cm (previously 1.0 cm). A new 0.6 cm hypodense focus is identified in segment V, which is new compared to the last study and is suspicious for an additional hepatic metastasis. There is no biliary ductal dilation. Mild hepatomegaly.

Lungs: The right lower lobe nodule has increased from 5 mm to 9 mm. The second right lower lobe nodule has grown from 4 mm to 6 mm. These findings are now considered highly suspicious for pulmonary metastases given the growth trajectory in the setting of progressive disease at other sites. No pleural effusion. No hilar or mediastinal adenopathy beyond prior baseline.

Lymph nodes: Right obturator lymph node now 2.4 cm, clearly enlarged from 1.6 cm on prior study. The retroperitoneal nodes (right para-aortic, aortocaval) appear stable. No new gross retroperitoneal adenopathy.

Bones: Not fully evaluated on non-bone scan CT; no cortical destruction or new aggressive lytic lesions seen on CT windows, though dedicated bone scan was not performed for this restaging interval. Clinical history and pain trajectory suggest stable-to-progressive osseous burden.

Other: Mild bilateral pleural thickening, trace fluid right greater than left. Adrenals unremarkable. No ascites.

Overall impression from CT: Progressive hepatic and pulmonary metastases and nodal disease despite 2 cycles of carboplatin + cabazitaxel.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

Mr. Kowalski is a 71-year-old gentleman with end-stage mCRPC, BRCA2 germline mutation, prior treatment with ADT plus abiraterone, enzalutamide, olaparib (dose-reduced due to anemia), and now carboplatin + cabazitaxel -- all of which have either failed or been discontinued due to toxicity. His disease has now progressed through 4 distinct lines of systemic therapy and has developed visceral involvement of the liver and lungs. His functional status is ECOG 3, he is transfusion-dependent, and his hepatic function, nutritional status, and renal function are all deteriorating.

1. Progressive, treatment-refractory mCRPC with visceral metastases (hepatic and pulmonary), nodal progression:

Restaging CT confirms clear disease progression through 2 cycles of reduced-dose carboplatin + cabazitaxel (PSA 18.6 ng/mL, rising; hepatic lesions growing and new lesion in segment V; pulmonary nodules significantly enlarged). This regimen is not providing disease control and is associated with substantial toxicity.

Further cytotoxic chemotherapy: I do not believe further chemotherapy is in Mr. Kowalski's best interest at this time. His performance status of ECOG 3, albumin of 2.7, rising bilirubin, compromised hepatic function, and progressive renal dysfunction (eGFR ~36) all make him a very poor candidate for additional systemic chemotherapy. The risk-benefit calculus is unfavorable. Cabazitaxel + carboplatin is being discontinued today.

Lutetium-177 PSMA-617 (Pluvicto): This was discussed. Prior PSMA imaging demonstrated PSMA avidity in the tumor burden. However, at this time, his hepatic compromise (elevated bilirubin, low albumin, rising ALP, and hepatic metastases) and ECOG 3 status represent relative-to-absolute contraindications for Lu-177 PSMA therapy. Hepatic dysfunction can significantly worsen radiopharmaceutical toxicity, and his marrow reserve is already severely compromised. This option is not safe to pursue at present.

Clinical trials: Our research team reviewed available trials for which he might be eligible. Given his hepatic dysfunction (bilirubin 1.8), renal function (eGFR ~36), ECOG 3, and prior treatment exposure, he does not meet eligibility criteria for any currently open protocols at our institution. No available trials identified.

ADT continuation: Leuprolide will be continued indefinitely as it likely provides some hormonal suppression at the tumor level and prevents testosterone flare. It is minimally burdensome to continue.

2. Transfusion-dependent anemia, hemoglobin 7.1 g/dL:
Ordering 2 units of pRBCs to be given today in the infusion center. This is his third transfusion within approximately 2 months. Given his trajectory, transfusion support will continue to be offered for symptom palliation on a case-by-case basis in coordination with his care team and per his preferences. This was discussed with the patient and family.

3. Pain management - severe, multifocal bone pain, 5/10 on current regimen:
Current opioid regimen of oxycodone ER 20 mg BID with oxycodone IR 5 mg Q4H PRN is providing incomplete pain control given 3-4 breakthrough doses per day. Palliative care and pain service have been actively co-managing. We discussed transitioning to transdermal fentanyl to improve compliance and simplify oral medication burden given his declining appetite and oral intake. Dose conversion and titration will be coordinated through the palliative care team and home hospice. Methadone as an alternative was discussed in brief but deferred to hospice/palliative care team.

4. Goals of care and hospice:
This was the central focus of today's visit. I spent significant time with Mr. Kowalski and his wife discussing the overall clinical picture. I clearly communicated that his cancer has grown despite our best available treatments, that the remaining systemic options carry high risk with very low likelihood of meaningful benefit, and that our focus at this point is most appropriately directed toward comfort, quality of life, time with family, and management of symptoms.

Mr. Kowalski was calm and thoughtful in this discussion. He stated that he has been thinking about this possibility and does not wish to pursue further aggressive cancer-directed therapies if they are unlikely to work. His primary goals are pain control, remaining at home, and spending time with his wife and adult children. He expressed that he does not want to spend further time in the hospital if avoidable.

Hospice referral: After detailed discussion with the patient and his family, Mr. Kowalski has agreed to transition to home hospice care. He meets clinical criteria for hospice enrollment based on his overall clinical trajectory, ECOG 3 (bordering on 4), progressive disease through multiple lines of therapy, visceral metastases, weight loss, hypoalbuminemia, and rising tumor markers. I explained the hospice philosophy, services available (nursing, aide support, social work, chaplaincy, medications for symptom management), and that hospice is not "giving up" but rather focusing our energy on what matters most to him.

DNR/DNI: The patient confirmed his prior advance directive. He does not wish resuscitation. He does not want to be intubated. He has designated his wife as durable power of attorney for health care. These decisions are documented.

We will initiate the hospice referral today. The social worker is being notified and will meet with the family before discharge to begin the enrollment process.

5. Hepatic dysfunction, rising bilirubin:
Likely directly attributable to hepatic metastases. No intervention planned beyond comfort-directed care. Signs of significant hepatic compromise (INR 1.4, bilirubin 1.8, hypoalbuminemia) are consistent with hepatic metastatic burden. In the hospice setting, routine lab monitoring will be discontinued per patient preference unless results would change management.

6. Hyponatremia (sodium 132 mEq/L):
Mild, likely multifactorial (poor oral intake, possible SIADH in the setting of malignancy). No acute intervention required. Will be monitored symptomatically in the hospice setting.

7. Chronic kidney disease, eGFR ~36:
Stable-to-slowly worsening. Metformin was discontinued at time of CRPC diagnosis given creatinine trends; not relevant to current management. Nephrotoxic agents to be avoided. No further intervention in comfort-focused framework.

8. Diabetes mellitus type 2:
Active glucose monitoring is of low priority in this setting. Antidiabetic medications will be simplified per hospice team recommendations.

9. Hypertension:
Blood pressure now 108/68 -- frankly low for this patient. Amlodipine and lisinopril to be dose-reduced or discontinued in coordination with hospice team. Risk of symptomatic hypotension outweighs benefit of continued antihypertensive therapy at this stage.

10. Follow-up:
Mr. Kowalski will be followed primarily by the home hospice team going forward. He is welcome to return to clinic for any symptom concerns or if his goals of care change. I will remain available to the hospice team for any oncology-related questions. A follow-up call from my nurse coordinator is scheduled in 48-72 hours to ensure smooth transition to hospice services and to confirm pain management optimization.

Total time spent with patient and family today: approximately 60 minutes, of which greater than 50% was counseling and care coordination.

Signed,
Attending Medical Oncologist
